terms.definition: Laws passed to establish the monarchs authority over the Church of England and enforce religious conformity.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Acts of Supremacy and Uniformity under Elizabeth I aimed to create a religious settlement.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the different Acts passed under Henry VIII and Elizabeth I

terms.definition: The scientific study of the structure of the human body, particularly through dissection.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Greek anatomia, a cutting up
terms.example_usage: Advances in anatomy during the Renaissance improved understanding of the human body.

terms.definition: A substance that reduces or eliminates pain during surgery, a key medical breakthrough.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The discovery of anesthetic in the 1840s made surgery less traumatic for patients.
terms.common_errors: Confusing anesthetic with antiseptic

terms.definition: A substance used to prevent infection by killing bacteria, pioneered by Joseph Lister.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Listers use of antiseptic carbolic acid dramatically reduced deaths from infection after surgery.
terms.common_errors: Confusing antiseptic with anesthetic

terms.definition: Physical remains such as buildings, objects, and bones that archaeologists study to learn about the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Archaeological evidence from the site revealed how people lived in medieval times.
terms.common_errors: Thinking archaeological evidence is always more reliable than written sources

terms.definition: A competition between nations to build up military strength, especially in weapons and nuclear capability.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The arms race between the USA and USSR was a defining feature of the Cold War.

terms.definition: The process by which a minority group adopts the customs, language, and identity of the majority culture.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin assimilare, to make similar
terms.example_usage: Government policies sometimes aimed at the forced assimilation of immigrant communities.
terms.common_errors: Confusing assimilation with integration, which preserves cultural identity

terms.definition: A travelling court in medieval and early modern England that heard serious criminal cases in different counties.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old French assise, a sitting
terms.example_usage: Assize courts brought royal justice to regions far from London.

terms.definition: A person who has left their country and is seeking protection from persecution in another country.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Asylum seekers fleeing persecution have arrived in Britain throughout its history.
terms.common_errors: Confusing asylum seeker with economic migrant

terms.definition: Economic self-sufficiency in which a country aims to produce everything it needs without relying on imports.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Nazi Germany pursued a policy of autarky to prepare for war.
terms.common_errors: Confused with autocracy, which means rule by one person

terms.definition: Presenting multiple viewpoints fairly without undue favouritism towards one side.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A balanced interpretation considers evidence from different perspectives.
terms.common_errors: Thinking balanced means giving equal weight to all views regardless of evidence

terms.definition: A nobleman of the lowest rank in the feudal system, holding land directly from the king in return for military service.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old French baron, man or warrior
terms.example_usage: Barons who were unhappy with King Johns rule forced him to sign Magna Carta.

terms.definition: The decisive battle in 1066 where William of Normandy defeated King Harold II to take control of England.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Battle of Hastings marked the beginning of Norman rule in England.

terms.definition: An 1876 battle in which Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors defeated US cavalry forces led by Custer.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Battle of Little Bighorn was a major Native American victory in the Plains Wars.

terms.definition: A 70-metre-long embroidered cloth depicting events surrounding the Norman Conquest of England in 1066.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after Bayeux, France, where it is kept
terms.example_usage: The Bayeux Tapestry provides a visual narrative of the events leading to the Battle of Hastings.
terms.common_errors: It is actually an embroidery, not a tapestry

terms.definition: A fortified barrier built in 1961 dividing East and West Berlin, symbolising the Cold War division of Europe.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolised the end of the Cold War.

terms.definition: The human-made structures and spaces in an area, including buildings, roads, and public spaces.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Studying the built environment of a town can reveal how it has changed over centuries.

terms.definition: A system of government administration carried out by appointed officials following set procedures.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The growth of bureaucracy helped empires like Chinas administer vast territories.
terms.common_errors: Always using bureaucracy negatively; it can also mean efficient administration

terms.definition: An Islamic state led by a caliph, a religious and political leader considered a successor to Muhammad.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Abbasid Caliphate became a centre of learning and culture in the medieval period.

terms.definition: The legally authorised killing of a person as punishment for a crime; the death penalty.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin capitalis, of the head
terms.example_usage: Capital punishment was used for many crimes in medieval England, including theft.
terms.common_errors: Confusing capital punishment with corporal punishment

terms.definition: An economic system based on private ownership of the means of production and operation for profit.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin capitalis, of the head or chief
terms.example_usage: The Cold War was partly a conflict between capitalism and communism.
terms.common_errors: Thinking capitalism means the same as democracy

terms.definition: A fortified building or complex built for defence, often serving as a residence for a lord or monarch.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Normans built castles across England to control the newly conquered population.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all castles look the same; designs changed over centuries

terms.definition: A factor or event that speeds up or triggers a process of change without being the sole cause.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The assassination served as a catalyst for the wider conflict that had been building for years.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a catalyst is the main cause rather than something that accelerates change

terms.definition: An event, person, or development that accelerates or initiates significant historical transformation.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The printing press was a catalyst for change in how knowledge was shared across Europe.

terms.definition: Relating to the Roman Catholic branch of Christianity, headed by the Pope in Rome.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Catholic worship was banned during the English Reformation under Edward VI.

terms.definition: The herding and moving of large numbers of cattle over long distances, common in the American West.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Cattle drives from Texas to the railheads in Kansas were central to the cowboy era.

terms.definition: The relationship between cause and effect; the process by which one event leads to another.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Understanding causation requires identifying which factors contributed to the outcome.
terms.common_errors: Confusing causation with correlation

terms.definition: A sequence of related events in which each one causes or leads to the next.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A chain of events led from the assassination of the Archduke to the outbreak of world war.

terms.definition: A controlled test of a medical treatment on patients to assess its safety and effectiveness.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Greek klinikos, of a bed
terms.example_usage: Modern clinical trials were developed to test whether new treatments actually worked.

terms.definition: The period of geopolitical tension between the USA and USSR (1947-1991) involving ideological rivalry but not direct military conflict.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Cold War shaped international relations for nearly half a century.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the Cold War involved no actual fighting; proxy wars did occur

terms.definition: Shared characteristics found across different periods, places, or events when making comparisons.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Common features of medieval and Tudor crime included petty theft and vagrancy.

terms.definition: A political community founded for the common good, or the period of republican government in England (1649-1660).
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: During the Commonwealth period, England was governed without a monarch.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the historical English Commonwealth with the modern Commonwealth of Nations

terms.definition: A political and economic ideology advocating collective ownership of the means of production and the abolition of private property.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The spread of communism after 1945 was a central concern of Western governments during the Cold War.
terms.common_errors: Thinking communism and socialism are identical

terms.definition: The examination of two or more things to identify similarities and differences between them.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin comparare, to pair together
terms.example_usage: A comparison of medieval and Tudor punishments reveals both change and continuity.
terms.common_errors: Only listing similarities without also identifying differences, or vice versa

terms.definition: An officer responsible for law enforcement, originally a medieval official appointed to keep the peace.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin comes stabuli, count of the stable
terms.example_usage: The role of the constable evolved from a medieval parish official to a professional police officer.

terms.definition: The US foreign policy strategy of preventing the spread of communism to new countries during the Cold War.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin continere, to hold together
terms.example_usage: The Truman Doctrine was a key expression of the policy of containment.

terms.definition: The importance or impact that an event or person had at the time it occurred, as judged by people living then.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The contemporary significance of the Reformation was enormous, splitting families and nations.
terms.common_errors: Confusing contemporary significance with later significance

terms.definition: A source created at the time of the events it describes, by someone who lived through them.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin contemporarius, of the same time
terms.example_usage: A contemporary source from 1066 gives us a first-hand account of the Norman invasion.
terms.common_errors: Confusing contemporary (of the time) with modern

terms.definition: The subject matter or information contained within a source, as distinct from its provenance or purpose.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: When analysing a source, consider both its content and who created it.

terms.definition: A noticeable difference between two or more things being compared.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin contra + stare, to stand against
terms.example_usage: There is a sharp contrast between medieval and modern approaches to crime and punishment.

terms.definition: One element among several that helped cause an event or outcome, without being the sole cause.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Poverty was a contributing factor to the rise of crime in Victorian cities.

terms.definition: The process of different things becoming more similar or coming together over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin convergere, to incline together
terms.example_usage: There was a convergence of religious and political motives behind the English Reformation.

terms.definition: Able to make someone believe that something is true or real, through strong evidence and reasoning.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The most convincing arguments are supported by a range of evidence from different sources.

terms.definition: Physical punishment inflicted on the body, such as whipping or branding, used historically as a penalty for crimes.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin corporalis, of the body
terms.example_usage: Corporal punishment was commonly used in medieval England for minor offences.
terms.common_errors: Confusing corporal with capital punishment

terms.definition: Confirmation of a claim or piece of evidence by comparing it with independent sources.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Corroboration from multiple sources increases our confidence in the reliability of the evidence.

terms.definition: An act that breaks the law of a society, with definitions varying significantly across historical periods.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: What counted as a crime changed over time; poaching was made a serious offence in the medieval period.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the definition of crime has stayed the same throughout history

terms.definition: A 13-day confrontation in 1962 between the USA and USSR over Soviet nuclear missiles placed in Cuba.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Cuban Missile Crisis brought the world closer to nuclear war than at any other time.

terms.definition: The sharing of ideas, customs, and traditions between different cultures through contact or interaction.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Cultural exchange along the Silk Road spread technologies and religions between East and West.

terms.definition: A build-up of multiple factors over time that together produce a significant event or change.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The outbreak of war resulted from cumulative causes that had been building for decades.

terms.definition: An 1887 US law that divided Native American tribal lands into individual allotments, undermining communal ownership.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Dawes Act aimed to assimilate Native Americans by breaking up tribal land.

terms.definition: The use of punishment or threat to discourage people from committing crimes or hostile actions.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin deterrere, to frighten away
terms.example_usage: Public executions were intended as a deterrence to others who might commit similar crimes.

terms.definition: The dispersion or spread of a people from their original homeland to other parts of the world.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Jewish diaspora spread communities across Europe and beyond over many centuries.

terms.definition: A way in which two or more things are not the same, identified through comparison.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: An important difference between medieval and modern justice is the presumption of innocence.

terms.definition: The unjust treatment of different categories of people, especially on the grounds of race, religion, or gender.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Discrimination against immigrant communities has been a recurring theme in British history.

terms.definition: The cutting open and examination of a dead body or organ, used in medical study and training.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin dissectio, a cutting apart
terms.example_usage: The legalisation of dissection allowed surgeons to learn anatomy from actual human bodies.

terms.definition: Characteristics that make something stand out or differ from others in a comparison.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Distinctive features of Norman castles include the motte-and-bailey design.

terms.definition: The process of becoming increasingly different or moving apart over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin divergere, to go in different directions
terms.example_usage: There was a divergence between Catholic and Protestant beliefs during the Reformation.

terms.definition: A comprehensive survey of English land and resources ordered by William the Conqueror in 1086.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From dome, meaning judgement (its findings were final)
terms.example_usage: The Domesday Book recorded who owned the land and how much it was worth.
terms.common_errors: Spelling it Doomsday; it is Domesday

terms.definition: Sir Francis Drake, an Elizabethan sea captain known for circumnavigating the globe and fighting the Spanish Armada.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Drake was both a national hero and a privateer who raided Spanish ships.

terms.definition: The quality of lasting over time; in historical significance, how long an events effects continue to be felt.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The durability of Roman roads shows the lasting significance of their engineering.

terms.definition: A succession of rulers from the same family who maintain power across generations.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Tudor dynasty ruled England from 1485 to 1603.

terms.definition: A period of reduced tension and improved relations between rival countries, especially during the Cold War.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Detente between the USA and USSR in the 1970s led to arms limitation agreements.
terms.common_errors: Thinking detente meant the end of the Cold War

terms.definition: Relating to the production, distribution, and consumption of goods, services, and wealth.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Economic factors such as trade and taxation were major causes of the American Revolution.

terms.definition: The act of leaving ones own country to settle permanently in another.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin emigrare, to move away
terms.example_usage: Irish emigration increased dramatically during the Great Famine of the 1840s.
terms.common_errors: Confusing emigration (leaving) with immigration (arriving)

terms.definition: A 1933 German law that gave Hitler the power to make laws without parliamentary approval, establishing dictatorship.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Enabling Act effectively ended democracy in Weimar Germany.

terms.definition: The study of how diseases spread within populations and what factors influence their distribution.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: John Snows work in epidemiology traced a cholera outbreak to a contaminated water pump.

terms.definition: Relying on verified evidence rather than tradition, assumption, or authority to draw conclusions.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Evidence-based medicine replaced older practices that relied on theory rather than testing.

terms.definition: The gradual development of something over time; in history, the slow process of change within institutions or ideas.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The evolution of policing from parish constables to professional forces took centuries.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with biological evolution

terms.definition: Something that does not follow the general pattern or rule, standing out as different from the norm.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin excipere, to take out
terms.example_usage: While most medieval punishments were harsh, pardons were an important exception.

terms.definition: The formal exclusion of a person from the sacraments and community of the Catholic Church by papal decree.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin excommunicare, to put out of community
terms.example_usage: The Popes excommunication of Henry VIII marked a turning point in the English Reformation.

terms.definition: The degree to which something is true, significant, or influential; how far a claim can be supported.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin extendere, to stretch out
terms.example_usage: To what extent was the Industrial Revolution driven by technological innovation?

terms.definition: The medieval social and economic system in which land was granted in exchange for loyalty and military service.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Feudalism created a rigid hierarchy from king to peasant based on land ownership.
terms.common_errors: Thinking feudalism worked exactly the same way everywhere

terms.definition: The edge of settled territory, especially the expanding western boundary of settlement in the American West.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old French frontiere, front line
terms.example_usage: Life on the frontier was shaped by conflict between settlers and Native Americans.

terms.definition: A broad statement or conclusion drawn from specific examples, which may not apply in every case.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Be careful about making generalisations; not all medieval peasants had the same experiences.
terms.common_errors: Making sweeping generalisations without acknowledging exceptions

terms.definition: The scientific theory that many diseases are caused by microorganisms, replacing the miasma theory.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Pasteurs germ theory transformed medical understanding and led to improvements in hygiene.
terms.common_errors: Thinking germ theory was immediately accepted

terms.definition: The secret state police of Nazi Germany, used to identify and suppress political opposition and enforce racial policies.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Gestapo used surveillance and informants to maintain control through fear.

terms.definition: A Native American spiritual movement of the late 1800s that promised the return of traditional ways and the departure of white settlers.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Ghost Dance movement frightened US authorities and contributed to the Wounded Knee massacre.

terms.definition: The increasing interconnection of the worlds economies, cultures, and populations through trade, migration, and technology.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Globalisation has historical roots in the trade networks established by medieval empires.
terms.common_errors: Thinking globalisation is only a modern phenomenon

terms.definition: Happening slowly over a period of time, rather than suddenly or all at once.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The shift from feudalism to a market economy was a gradual process spanning centuries.

terms.definition: The devastating military campaign by William the Conqueror to crush resistance in northern England in 1069-1070.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old English hergian, to ravage
terms.example_usage: The Harrying of the North destroyed villages and farmland, causing widespread famine.

terms.definition: The physical surroundings shaped by past human activity, including buildings, landscapes, and archaeological sites.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The historic environment of a town can be studied to understand how it developed over time.

terms.definition: A disagreement between historians about the interpretation of events, causes, or significance.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: There is ongoing historical debate about whether the Norman Conquest was positive or negative for England.

terms.definition: The extent to which a past event, person, or development connects to or explains current issues.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The historical relevance of the slave trade is evident in ongoing discussions about racial equality.

terms.definition: The degree to which a past event, person, or development had a lasting impact or changed the course of history.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Historians use criteria such as impact and durability to assess historical significance.
terms.common_errors: Confusing significance with fame

terms.definition: Having had a notable impact on the course of events or the development of society.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The invention of the printing press was historically significant because it transformed how knowledge spread.

terms.definition: The study of how history has been written and interpreted by different historians over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Historiography reveals how interpretations of the British Empire have changed over the decades.
terms.common_errors: Confusing historiography with history itself

terms.definition: Adolf Hitler, leader of the Nazi Party who became dictator of Germany and instigated World War Two and the Holocaust.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Hitler exploited economic crisis and resentment to rise to power in Germany.

terms.definition: The youth organisation of the Nazi Party, used to indoctrinate young Germans with Nazi ideology.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Membership of the Hitler Youth became compulsory for all German boys by 1939.

terms.definition: A formal public pledge of allegiance from a vassal to a lord in the feudal system.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old French hommage, from homme (man)
terms.example_usage: A vassal would pay homage to his lord by kneeling and swearing loyalty.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the modern meaning of paying tribute

terms.definition: An 1862 US law granting 160 acres of public land to settlers who farmed it for five years.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Homestead Act encouraged thousands of families to settle on the Great Plains.

terms.definition: The existing population in an area where migrants arrive and settle.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Relations between immigrant groups and the host community have varied throughout British history.

terms.definition: A French Protestant, many of whom fled religious persecution in France and settled in England and other countries.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Huguenot refugees brought valuable silk-weaving skills to London in the 17th century.

terms.definition: Extremely rapid and uncontrolled rises in prices that destroy the value of a currency.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Hyperinflation in Weimar Germany in 1923 made money virtually worthless.
terms.common_errors: Confusing hyperinflation with ordinary inflation

terms.definition: Relating to a system of ideas and beliefs, especially those that form the basis of a political or economic theory.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Cold War was fundamentally an ideological conflict between capitalism and communism.

terms.definition: Happening directly and without delay; in history, the most direct and proximate cause or effect.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The immediate cause of the war was the assassination, but underlying tensions had existed for years.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the immediate cause is the most important cause

terms.definition: The process of people arriving in and settling in a country that is not their native land.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin immigrare, to move in
terms.example_usage: Immigration from the Commonwealth transformed British society in the 20th century.
terms.common_errors: Confusing immigration (arriving) with emigration (leaving)

terms.definition: The court and administration of an emperor or empress, serving as the centre of government.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The imperial court of the Chinese Tang dynasty was a centre of art and scholarship.

terms.definition: The punishment of confining someone in prison, which became increasingly common from the 19th century.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Imprisonment replaced corporal punishment as the main form of punishment during the 19th century.

terms.definition: Happening through small, gradual steps rather than sudden large changes.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Medical knowledge improved through incremental advances over several centuries.

terms.definition: Originating in or native to a particular place; the original inhabitants of a region.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Indigenous peoples of the Great Plains had lived there for thousands of years before European settlers arrived.

terms.definition: A conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning rather than explicit statements.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin inferre, to bring in
terms.example_usage: We can draw an inference about living conditions from the types of objects found at the site.
terms.common_errors: Confusing inference with assumption; inferences should be evidence-based

terms.definition: The process of combining people from different backgrounds into a unified community while maintaining cultural identities.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin integrare, to make whole
terms.example_usage: Integration of immigrant communities has happened at different rates throughout British history.
terms.common_errors: Confusing integration with assimilation

terms.definition: Planned or deliberately aimed at; consequences that were foreseen and desired by the people involved.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin intendere, to aim at
terms.example_usage: The intended consequence of the reform was to reduce poverty, but it also had unintended effects.

terms.definition: A formal ceremony conferring authority or a position of power, especially in the feudal or religious context.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Investiture Contest was a dispute between popes and emperors over the appointment of bishops.

terms.definition: A mounted warrior in the feudal system who served a lord in exchange for land, following a code of chivalry.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old English cniht, boy or servant
terms.example_usage: Knights were expected to provide military service to their lord in return for their estates.
terms.common_errors: Romanticised image of knights from fiction vs historical reality

terms.definition: The visible features of an area including physical landforms and human-made structures that reveal historical layers.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The landscape of a medieval town tells us about its defences, trade, and social organisation.

terms.definition: The system and personnel responsible for maintaining public order and ensuring compliance with the law.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Law enforcement has evolved from community-based systems to professional police forces.

terms.definition: A restriction or shortcoming of a source or piece of evidence that affects what we can learn from it.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A limitation of this source is that it only represents the views of the ruling class.
terms.common_errors: Thinking limitations make a source useless

terms.definition: A particular area or neighbourhood, especially one studied in detail for its historical features.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A locality study of our area reveals how it changed during the Industrial Revolution.

terms.definition: A factor that develops over months, years, or decades and contributes to an eventual event.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Rivalry between European empires was a long-term cause of World War One.
terms.common_errors: Confusing long-term causes with triggers

terms.definition: The lasting importance or impact of an event measured over years, decades, or centuries.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The long-term significance of the printing press was the spread of literacy across Europe.

terms.definition: A breed of cattle with long horns, central to the cattle industry of the American West.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Texas longhorn cattle were driven north to railway towns for sale in eastern markets.

terms.definition: A judicial officer who deals with minor offences and preliminary hearings in a local court.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Magistrates have been responsible for local justice in England since the medieval period.

terms.definition: The 19th-century belief that American settlers were destined to expand across the North American continent.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Coined by journalist John OSullivan in 1845
terms.example_usage: The idea of Manifest Destiny was used to justify the displacement of Native Americans.

terms.definition: A US programme of economic aid to Western Europe after World War Two, aimed at rebuilding economies and preventing communism.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after US Secretary of State George Marshall
terms.example_usage: The Marshall Plan helped rebuild Western European economies and strengthened ties with the USA.

terms.definition: The physical objects, artefacts, and structures created by a society that reveal how people lived.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Material culture from Anglo-Saxon graves tells us about their beliefs and craftsmanship.

terms.definition: The pre-modern belief that diseases were caused by bad air or foul smells from rotting matter.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The miasma theory was eventually disproved by the germ theory of disease.

terms.definition: A significant event or achievement that marks an important stage in a process of development.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Originally a stone marking distance on a road
terms.example_usage: The discovery of penicillin was a milestone in the history of medicine.

terms.definition: A structure or building erected to commemorate a notable person or event from the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The monument in the town square commemorates those who died in the World Wars.

terms.definition: An early type of castle consisting of an earth mound (motte) topped with a wooden tower and an enclosed courtyard (bailey).
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Normans quickly built motte-and-bailey castles to establish control after the Conquest.

terms.definition: The presence of and respect for diverse cultural and ethnic groups within a society.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Britains multiculturalism has been shaped by centuries of immigration from around the world.

terms.definition: The doctrine that nuclear war would result in the total destruction of both attacker and defender, deterring first strikes.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Mutually assured destruction meant that neither superpower could launch a nuclear attack without being destroyed in return.

terms.definition: The North Atlantic Treaty Organization, a Western military alliance formed in 1949 to counter Soviet expansion.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: NATO was established to provide collective defence against potential Soviet aggression.

terms.definition: The essential character or type of something; in history, used to describe what kind of change or event occurred.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin natura, birth or character
terms.example_usage: The nature of the change was political rather than economic.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the environmental meaning

terms.definition: The characteristics and type of a historical change, such as whether it was political, social, economic, sudden, or gradual.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Understanding the nature of change helps explain why the Industrial Revolution affected different groups differently.

terms.definition: The National Socialist German Workers Party, which ruled Germany 1933-1945 under Adolf Hitlers leadership.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Nazi Party exploited economic hardship and nationalism to gain support in Weimar Germany.

terms.definition: The National Health Service, established in 1948 to provide free healthcare to all British citizens at the point of use.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The creation of the NHS was a landmark in the history of public health in Britain.

terms.definition: The invasion and takeover of England by William, Duke of Normandy, following the Battle of Hastings in 1066.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Norman Conquest transformed English society, language, and governance.

terms.definition: The German abbreviation for the National Socialist German Workers Party (the Nazi Party).
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The NSDAP grew from a small party to control all aspects of German life by the late 1930s.

terms.definition: A subtle difference in meaning, expression, or interpretation that adds complexity to an argument.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From French nuance, shade of colour
terms.example_usage: A nuanced answer acknowledges that there are multiple valid perspectives on the question.

terms.definition: The strategy of maintaining nuclear weapons to discourage enemies from attacking, based on the threat of retaliation.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Nuclear deterrence was the central strategy of both superpowers during the Cold War.

terms.definition: Antisemitic laws enacted in Nazi Germany in 1935 that stripped Jewish people of citizenship and civil rights.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after the city of Nuremberg
terms.example_usage: The Nuremberg Laws were a key step in the escalation of Nazi persecution of Jewish people.

terms.definition: Presenting only one viewpoint or perspective, without considering alternative interpretations.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The account is one-sided because it was written by the victors.

terms.definition: Cultural knowledge, stories, and beliefs passed down through generations by spoken word rather than writing.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Many Native American histories were preserved through oral tradition rather than written records.
terms.common_errors: Thinking oral traditions are unreliable because they are not written down

terms.definition: The traditional or most widely accepted explanation of a historical event or period.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The orthodox interpretation of the Cold War blamed Soviet expansion, but revisionist historians challenged this.

terms.definition: The speed at which change or events take place; whether a process is rapid, gradual, or uneven.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The pace of industrialisation varied significantly between different regions of Britain.

terms.definition: A comparison between two events, periods, or developments that share similar characteristics.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Greek parallelos, beside one another
terms.example_usage: There are parallels between the rise of Nazi Germany and other authoritarian regimes.

terms.definition: The support, encouragement, or financial backing given by a wealthy or powerful person to an individual or cause.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Royal patronage was essential for scientific and artistic achievement in the Tudor period.

terms.definition: Relating to the preparation, dispensing, and use of medicinal drugs.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The pharmaceutical industry transformed medicine in the 20th century with mass-produced treatments.

terms.definition: Tangible objects and remains that can be examined and studied to learn about the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Physical evidence from the battlefield helped historians reconstruct what happened during the battle.

terms.definition: The various Native American peoples who lived on the Great Plains of North America.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Plains Indians developed a way of life centred on the buffalo and horse.
terms.common_errors: Treating Plains Indians as a single homogeneous group

terms.definition: An organised massacre or persecution of an ethnic or religious group, especially Jewish communities.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Pogroms in Russia and Eastern Europe drove many Jewish families to emigrate in the late 19th century.

terms.definition: The system and methods by which law and order are maintained in a society.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The history of policing shows a shift from community watch systems to professional forces.

terms.definition: Relating to the governance and power structures of a state or society.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Political factors, including rivalry between monarchs, contributed to the outbreak of war.

terms.definition: Relating to the period before a territory came under colonial rule by a foreign power.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Pre-colonial African societies had complex systems of government and trade.
terms.common_errors: Assuming pre-colonial societies were primitive

terms.definition: The act of maintaining and protecting historical buildings, sites, and artefacts for future generations.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The preservation of the historic environment allows us to learn from the physical remains of the past.

terms.definition: A body of advisors to the monarch, historically a key organ of government in Tudor and Stuart England.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Elizabeth Is Privy Council helped her manage the complex politics of the religious settlement.

terms.definition: Royal tours of the country undertaken by Elizabeth I to display her power and secure the loyalty of her subjects.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Elizabeths progresses through southern England were elaborate displays of royal authority.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the general meaning of making progress

terms.definition: Information, often biased or misleading, used to promote a political cause or viewpoint.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Nazi propaganda used posters, film, and radio to spread antisemitic ideas.
terms.common_errors: Thinking propaganda is always lies; it can use selective truths

terms.definition: An electoral system in which parties gain seats in proportion to the number of votes they receive.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Proportional representation in the Weimar Republic led to many small parties in the Reichstag.

terms.definition: Relating to the branches of Christianity that broke away from the Roman Catholic Church during the Reformation.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin protestari, to declare publicly
terms.example_usage: Protestant reformers challenged Catholic teachings and practices in the 16th century.

terms.definition: A conflict in which two opposing powers support different sides without directly fighting each other.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Korean War was a proxy war between the USA and the Soviet Union.

terms.definition: The health of the population as a whole, especially as managed by government through sanitation, medicine, and regulation.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Improvements in public health during the 19th century included clean water supplies and sewage systems.

terms.definition: A positive condition in a destination that attracts migrants to move there, such as jobs or safety.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Economic opportunities in Britain were a pull factor for Caribbean migrants after 1948.
terms.common_errors: Confusing pull factors with push factors

terms.definition: A penalty imposed for breaking the law, varying greatly across historical periods in severity and purpose.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Punishment in the medieval period was often public and designed to deter others.

terms.definition: A Protestant Christian who sought to purify the Church of England of remaining Catholic practices.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From pure, seeking purity of worship
terms.example_usage: Puritans opposed the religious settlement under Elizabeth I, wanting simpler forms of worship.

terms.definition: The reason why a source was created; understanding purpose helps assess reliability and usefulness.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The purpose of the propaganda poster was to persuade people to support the war effort.

terms.definition: A negative condition in a home area that drives people to leave, such as poverty, war, or persecution.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Religious persecution was a push factor for Huguenot migration to England.
terms.common_errors: Confusing push factors with pull factors

terms.definition: Conflicts in the American West between cattle ranchers, sheep farmers, and homesteaders over land use.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Range wars erupted as different groups competed for control of open grazing land.

terms.definition: Happening quickly or in a short period of time, as opposed to gradual change.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The rapid growth of factories during the Industrial Revolution transformed British cities.

terms.definition: The process of rebuilding or reorganising after a conflict or major event; also the historical study of recreating past environments.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Reconstruction of the medieval village was based on archaeological evidence from the site.

terms.definition: A person who refused to attend Church of England services, especially Roman Catholics during the Elizabethan period.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Recusants faced heavy fines and imprisonment for refusing to conform to the religious settlement.

terms.definition: The 16th-century religious movement that led to the break from the Roman Catholic Church and the creation of Protestant churches.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Reformation in England began when Henry VIII broke with Rome over his divorce.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the Reformation was only about religion; it had major political and economic dimensions

terms.definition: A person who has been forced to leave their country to escape war, persecution, or natural disaster.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From French refugie, gone to shelter
terms.example_usage: Jewish refugees fleeing Nazi persecution sought safety in Britain and other countries.
terms.common_errors: Confusing refugee with economic migrant

terms.definition: The process of helping offenders reform and reintegrate into society, as a purpose of punishment.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Modern prisons aim to balance rehabilitation with deterrence and retribution.

terms.definition: The German parliament building in Berlin, and by extension the parliament itself during the Weimar and Nazi periods.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Reichstag Fire in 1933 was used by Hitler as a pretext to suspend civil liberties.

terms.definition: The degree to which a source can be trusted to provide accurate and truthful information about the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old French relier, to depend on
terms.example_usage: We must assess the reliability of every source by considering its provenance and purpose.
terms.common_errors: Thinking any single source is completely reliable or completely unreliable

terms.definition: The compromise arrangement for the Church of England established under Elizabeth I, balancing Catholic and Protestant elements.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Elizabeths religious settlement aimed to find a middle way that most English people could accept.

terms.definition: An area of land set aside by the US government for Native Americans, often far from their original territories.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin reservare, to keep back
terms.example_usage: Native Americans were forced onto reservations as settlers took over their traditional lands.

terms.definition: Opposition to or reluctance to accept new ideas, technologies, or social transformations.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Resistance to change from traditional healers slowed the acceptance of germ theory.

terms.definition: Punishment inflicted as vengeance for a wrongdoing, focused on making the offender suffer.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin retribuere, to pay back
terms.example_usage: Medieval punishments were often based on retribution rather than rehabilitation.

terms.definition: A historian who challenges the established or orthodox interpretation of a historical event or period.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin revidere, to look again
terms.example_usage: Revisionist historians argued that the Cold War was not solely caused by Soviet aggression.
terms.common_errors: Thinking revisionist means wrong; it means offering an alternative interpretation

terms.definition: A situation where one event causes a series of further events, spreading outward like ripples in water.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Black Death had a ripple effect on wages, land ownership, and social mobility.

terms.definition: The Sturmabteilung, the Nazi Partys paramilitary wing used to intimidate opponents in the early years.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The SA used violence against political opponents and Jewish businesses during the early 1930s.
terms.common_errors: Confusing SA with SS

terms.definition: The size, extent, or scope of an event or change; how many people or places were affected.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The scale of the Holocaust was unprecedented, with six million Jewish people murdered.

terms.definition: Elizabethan privateers and sea captains who raided Spanish ships and colonies with unofficial royal approval.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The sea dogs, including Drake and Hawkins, enriched England while weakening Spanish power.

terms.definition: A Catholic priest trained in secret seminaries abroad who returned to England to maintain Catholic worship.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Seminary priests faced execution if caught in Elizabethan England.

terms.definition: A form of military government in Japan led by a shogun, who held power while the emperor remained a figurehead.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Tokugawa Shogunate ruled Japan for over 250 years until the Meiji Restoration.

terms.definition: A factor that occurs close in time to an event and directly triggers it, as opposed to long-term causes.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand was a short-term cause of World War One.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the short-term cause is the most important cause

terms.definition: A way in which two or more things are alike, identified through comparison.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A key similarity between Roman and modern roads is that both were designed for efficient travel.

terms.definition: A group of Native American peoples, including the Lakota and Dakota, who lived on the Great Plains.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Ojibwe nadouessioux, little snakes (considered a misnomer)
terms.example_usage: The Sioux fought to defend their territory against US government expansion and gold prospectors.
terms.common_errors: Treating the Sioux as a single tribe rather than a group of related nations

terms.definition: A specific location where historical events took place or where physical remains can be studied.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The archaeological site revealed the foundations of a medieval village.

terms.definition: Relating to the organisation and relationships within a society, including class, community, and everyday life.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Social changes after the Black Death included greater freedom for peasants.

terms.definition: The fleet of 130 ships sent by Spain in 1588 to invade England, which was defeated by English forces and storms.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The defeat of the Spanish Armada secured Elizabeths position and boosted English national confidence.

terms.definition: The disproved belief that living organisms could arise from non-living matter, such as maggots from rotting meat.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Pasteurs experiments disproved spontaneous generation and supported germ theory.

terms.definition: The Schutzstaffel, the Nazi paramilitary organisation responsible for concentration camps, mass killings, and political terror.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The SS ran the concentration and extermination camps during the Holocaust.
terms.common_errors: Confusing SS with SA

terms.definition: A period of little or no growth, progress, or development.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin stagnare, to be still
terms.example_usage: The medieval period saw relative stagnation in medical knowledge compared to earlier Roman advances.

terms.definition: Gustav Stresemann, German chancellor and foreign minister who stabilised the Weimar Republic in the mid-1920s.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Stresemanns policies brought economic recovery and international cooperation to Weimar Germany.

terms.definition: The order in which people inherit a throne, title, or position of power.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin successio, a following after
terms.example_usage: The question of succession was a major political issue throughout the Tudor period.

terms.definition: A state or territory ruled by a sultan, a Muslim sovereign.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Delhi Sultanate was a powerful Islamic state in medieval India.

terms.definition: Attributed to forces beyond natural or scientific explanation, such as God, the devil, or spirits.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Medieval people often attributed disease to supernatural causes such as Gods punishment.

terms.definition: A nation with dominant political, economic, and military power on a global scale.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: After 1945, the USA and USSR emerged as the two rival superpowers.

terms.definition: Medical treatment involving physical intervention on the body, such as cutting, stitching, or removing tissue.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Surgery became safer after the introduction of anesthetics and antiseptics in the 19th century.

terms.definition: A system of routes and relationships through which goods, ideas, and culture are exchanged between different regions.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Medieval trade networks connected Europe with Asia and Africa through the Silk Road.

terms.definition: A railway line spanning the North American continent, completed in 1869, connecting the east and west coasts.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The transcontinental railroad transformed settlement and trade in the American West.

terms.definition: A thorough or dramatic change in form, structure, or character.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Industrial Revolution brought about a transformation of British society and economy.

terms.definition: The punishment of sending convicted criminals to penal colonies abroad, especially Australia.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin transportare, to carry across
terms.example_usage: Transportation to Australia was used as a punishment for serious crimes in the 18th and 19th centuries.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the general meaning of moving goods or people

terms.definition: Treaties (1851 and 1868) between the US government and Plains Indian nations defining territorial boundaries.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Treaty of Fort Laramie promised the Sioux their sacred Black Hills, but the US later broke this agreement.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the 1851 and 1868 treaties

terms.definition: A system in which weaker states or peoples pay goods or services to a more powerful ruler in exchange for protection or peace.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Chinese tribute system required neighbouring states to acknowledge the emperors authority.

terms.definition: The 1947 US policy of providing military and economic aid to countries resisting communist influence.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after US President Harry Truman
terms.example_usage: The Truman Doctrine committed the USA to containing the spread of communism worldwide.

terms.definition: A moment or event that marks a decisive change in the direction of events or in the course of history.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Battle of Stalingrad was a turning point in World War Two on the Eastern Front.

terms.definition: A deep-rooted factor that contributes to an event over the long term, operating beneath the surface of events.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Economic inequality was an underlying cause of the French Revolution.

terms.definition: The degree to which a source helps answer a particular historical question or line of enquiry.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The usefulness of a source depends on the question being asked, not just its reliability.
terms.common_errors: Confusing usefulness with reliability; a biased source can still be useful

terms.definition: The administration of a weakened or inactive form of a disease to build immunity, pioneered by Edward Jenner.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin vacca, cow (Jenner used cowpox)
terms.example_usage: Jenners vaccination against smallpox was one of the greatest breakthroughs in medical history.
terms.common_errors: Confusing vaccination with inoculation, which uses the actual disease

terms.definition: Differences or changes within a general pattern or trend, showing that experiences were not uniform.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: There was significant variation in how different regions experienced the Industrial Revolution.

terms.definition: A person in the feudal system who held land from a lord in return for loyalty and military service.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A vassal owed military service to his lord and could lose his land if he broke his oath.

terms.definition: A particular perspective or way of looking at an issue, influenced by a persons beliefs, experiences, or position.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Different viewpoints on the British Empire depend on whose experiences are centred.

terms.definition: A military alliance of communist states in Eastern Europe, formed in 1955 as a counterpart to NATO.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after Warsaw, where the treaty was signed
terms.example_usage: The Warsaw Pact bound Eastern European nations into a Soviet-led military alliance.

terms.definition: The democratic government of Germany from 1919 to 1933, established after World War One and ended by the Nazi seizure of power.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after the city of Weimar where the constitution was drafted
terms.example_usage: The Weimar Republic faced economic crises and political instability throughout its existence.

terms.definition: The ship HMT Empire Windrush, which brought Caribbean migrants to Britain in 1948, symbolising post-war immigration.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Windrush generation made significant contributions to British society and culture.

terms.definition: The council of advisors to Anglo-Saxon kings, composed of nobles, bishops, and other leading figures.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old English witena gemot, meeting of wise men
terms.example_usage: The Witan played a role in choosing the next king when there was no clear heir.
